refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control architecture with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and communication bottlenecks.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDI) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Implement configuration management and rapid reconfiguration capability to restore system to known-good state without full restart.
DP8: Design for testability with built-in self-test (BIST) and health-check mechanisms to continuously validate subsystem readiness.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 (integrated approach): Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover, establish predictive maintenance via condition monitoring, and deploy comprehensive fault detection/isolation with automated diagnostics. This combination addresses both reactive recovery (DP1) and proactive prevention (DP3) while minimizing MTTR through intelligent FDI (DP6).

potential_sources: DP1 (redundancy) and DP3 (predictive maintenance) are loosely coupled: redundancy masks failures while predictive maintenance prevents them—both needed but require clear ownership boundaries (e.g., redundancy logic vs. condition-monitoring algorithms).
DP6 (FDI) is tightly coupled to DP1: failover decisions depend on accurate fault isolation; poor FDI can trigger false failovers, degrading availability.
Synchronization between redundant subsystems (DP1) and condition monitoring (DP3) must be explicit to avoid stale health data triggering incorrect failover decisions.
DP1 introduces complexity in state consistency across redundant units; this couples with DP7 (reconfiguration) if reconfiguration logic differs between primary and backup.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable if clear module boundaries are established: (1) Redundancy control module owns failover logic and state synchronization; (2) Condition monitoring module owns sensor fusion and degradation models; (3) FDI module owns fault classification and isolation rules. Interfaces between modules must be well-defined (e.g., health status API, failover trigger protocol).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be kept in synchronized state with acceptable overhead; RISK: Synchronization latency or failures could cause both units to fail simultaneously, negating redundancy benefit.

ASSUMPTION: Condition monitoring sensors and algorithms are sufficiently reliable to predict failures; RISK: Sensor drift, calibration errors, or model inaccuracy could lead to false positives (unnecessary maintenance) or false negatives (missed failures).
ASSUMPTION: Fault detection and isolation can be performed with low false-positive rate; RISK: Excessive false alarms erode operator trust and increase unnecessary failovers, reducing availability.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite environment permits installation and maintenance of redundant hardware/sensors; RISK: Space, power, or environmental constraints may limit redundancy feasibility.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is sufficiently long to observe failure modes and validate predictive models; RISK: Short evaluation windows may not capture rare failure modes, leading to optimistic availability estimates.
ASSUMPTION: Operator training and procedures support rapid response to FDI alerts; RISK: Inadequate training or unclear procedures could delay repairs, increasing downtime.
ASSUMPTION: Spare parts and replacement components are available on-site or with acceptable lead times; RISK: Supply chain delays could extend MTTR beyond acceptable limits.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly decompose availability into two sub-requirements: (1) FR-A: Prevent failures through predictive maintenance and design robustness (target: reduce failure rate by X%); (2) FR-B: Recover rapidly from unavoidable failures through redundancy and FDI (target: reduce MTTR to ≤ Y hours). This separation clarifies the role of each DP and enables independent evaluation. Additionally, add explicit FR for 'Maintain synchronized state across redundant subsystems' to surface the coupling between DP1 and DP3, and introduce FR for 'Provide operator visibility into system health and fault status' to ensure FDI (DP6) is actionable. Finally, quantify the evaluation period and specify failure mode coverage expectations to reduce assumption risk.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized fault detection, isolation, diagnostics, redundancy management, predictive maintenance monitoring, and automated failover orchestration for critical vehicle subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingest real-time sensor data, subsystem health metrics, and condition indicators from Vehicle for fault detection and predictive analysis.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit fault notifications, failover events, maintenance alerts, and diagnostic summaries to external safety-critical consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive operational context and constraints; send failover directives and subsystem reconfiguration commands to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate with remote diagnostics, predictive maintenance services, and redundancy coordination in distributed architectures.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: FDI, diagnostics, redundancy management, and predictive maintenance are core safety functions. Fault detection logic, failover orchestration, and condition monitoring naturally cohere within safety domain. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and tasking/alert egress cross boundaries.

Poor fit: Vehicle is the subject of monitoring and control, not the owner of fault detection/diagnostics logic. Placing DP1+DP3+DP6 here creates tight coupling between subsystem implementation and fault logic, violates separation of concerns, and makes diagnostics hard to evolve independently. High diffusion: redundancy and predictive logic would leak into multiple subsystem modules.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is operator interface and remote control, not fault management. FDI, redundancy, and predictive maintenance are orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. Placing DP1+DP3+DP6 here creates severe diffusion: logic would need to propagate to Vehicle, Safety Services, and external diagnostics. Violates single responsibility.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system integrator or platform. While it could host orchestration, the detailed FDI algorithms, condition monitoring, and redundancy state machines belong in a more specialized safety-focused module. Placing DP1+DP3+DP6 here dilutes NGHE's cohesion and creates diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and external services.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural owner of integrated fault detection, isolation, diagnostics, redundancy management, and predictive maintenance because: (1) these are inherently safety-critical functions; (2) they require a unified view of system health across all subsystems; (3) they must operate independently of operational mode (teleoperation vs. autonomous); (4) they enable centralized policy for failover and maintenance scheduling; (5) they minimize MTTR through co-located logic for detection and response.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services may become a bottleneck if redundancy failover requires sub-millisecond response. Mitigation: Implement local fault detection in Vehicle with Safety Services as orchestrator and policy authority.

Risk: Predictive maintenance (DP3) may require domain-specific models per subsystem. Mitigation: Safety Services owns the monitoring framework and alert logic; Vehicle subsystems provide condition data and accept maintenance directives.
Risk: Network latency in distributed architectures may delay FDI. Mitigation: Partition FDI into local (Vehicle) and remote (Safety Services) layers; Safety Services coordinates global state.
Risk: Teleoperation may need to override failover decisions. Mitigation: Define clear escalation paths via Tasking interface; Safety Services retains veto on safety-critical failovers.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision needed. DP1 + DP3 + DP6 is well-scoped and cohesive as stated. Safety Services is the correct boundary. If latency or distribution becomes a concern, consider a two-tier architecture: (1) Safety Services owns policy, diagnostics, and predictive models; (2) Vehicle hosts a lightweight local FDI agent that executes Safety Services policies in real-time. This preserves logical cohesion while improving response time.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 (redundancy + predictive maintenance + FDI/diagnostics) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR requirement to achieve and maintain availability ≥ 0.97. The combination is logically sound: reactive recovery (DP1) handles failures post-occurrence; proactive prevention (DP3) reduces failure frequency; intelligent FDI (DP6) minimizes MTTR. No logical gaps detected between functional requirement and design parameters.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Safety Services may become a bottleneck for sub-millisecond failover response if all FDI logic is centralized. Mitigation: Implement two-tier architecture with local Vehicle-hosted FDI agents executing Safety Services policies in real-time.

Risk: Predictive maintenance (DP3) domain-specific models may leak into Vehicle subsystem modules if not carefully abstracted. Mitigation: Safety Services owns monitoring framework and alert logic; Vehicle provides only condition data feeds.

Risk: Network latency in distributed architectures may delay FDI decisions. Mitigation: Partition FDI into local (Vehicle) detection and remote (Safety Services) orchestration; Safety Services maintains global state authority.

Risk: Teleoperation override of failover decisions could compromise safety. Mitigation: Define escalation paths via Tasking interface; Safety Services retains veto authority on safety-critical failovers.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal IM boundary with 0.92 containment score and 0.08 diffusion score. FDI, diagnostics, redundancy management, and predictive maintenance are inherently cohesive safety-critical functions that naturally belong in a centralized, specialized module. Interfaces are well-defined: Telemetry (in), SafetyAlert (out), Tasking (inout), Network Link (inout). Alternative placements (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) show poor containment (0.55, 0.2, 0.35 respectively) with high diffusion risk (0.45, 0.8, 0.65). No revision to DP or IM boundary is required.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Availability measurement is directly observable via operational telemetry ingested through the Telemetry interface. Safety Services can emit availability metrics and fault event logs via SafetyAlert. Acceptance criterion (≥0.97 availability over agreed evaluation period) is measurable and traceable to DP1 (redundancy uptime), DP3 (failure prevention rate), and DP6 (MTTR reduction). Worksite safety plan and operational policies can be enforced through Tasking interface constraints and Safety Services policy engine.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services owns the integrated responsibility for fault detection, isolation, diagnostics, redundancy orchestration, and predictive maintenance. Vehicle is the subject of monitoring and control, not the owner of fault logic. Teleoperation is the operator interface, not the fault manager. NGHE Solution is the system integrator. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous and support independent evolution of each module.

recommended_revisions: No revision to FR, DP, or IM boundary is required. The mapping is sound and well-justified.

Optional enhancement: If latency analysis reveals that centralized Safety Services FDI cannot meet sub-millisecond failover SLAs, implement a two-tier architecture: (1) Safety Services owns policy, diagnostics algorithms, and predictive models; (2) Vehicle hosts a lightweight local FDI agent that executes Safety Services policies in real-time. This preserves logical cohesion while improving response time and reducing network dependency.
Ensure Tasking interface contract explicitly defines failover decision escalation paths and Safety Services veto authority on safety-critical actions to prevent Teleoperation override conflicts.

Document condition monitoring data schema and predictive maintenance model interfaces between Vehicle subsystems and Safety Services to prevent diffusion of domain-specific logic into Vehicle modules.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence and clear ownership boundaries. However, the gate results explicitly identify latency-critical sub-problems and architectural partitioning concerns that warrant decomposition into next-level sub-FRs. Specifically: (1) sub-millisecond failover response time is a distinct functional requirement not fully addressed at this level; (2) two-tier FDI architecture (local Vehicle agents + centralized Safety Services) requires explicit functional decomposition to clarify responsibilities and interfaces; (3) predictive maintenance model abstraction and condition data schema need functional clarity to prevent diffusion. The recommended enhancement for two-tier architecture is a strong signal that the current FR is logically sound but architecturally incomplete. Decomposition will expose latency, distribution, and data abstraction requirements as separate FRs, enabling clearer DP and IM allocation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Detect and isolate faults in real-time (sub-millisecond latency) at the Vehicle level without dependency on remote Safety Services communication.
FR1.2: Orchestrate failover decisions and maintain global fault state authority at the Safety Services level with deterministic escalation to Teleoperation when human intervention is required.
FR1.3: Predict incipient failures and recommend preventive maintenance actions based on condition monitoring data without leaking domain-specific models into Vehicle subsystem modules.
FR1.4: Enforce worksite safety policies and operator constraints on failover decisions through the Tasking interface while preserving Safety Services veto authority on safety-critical actions.
FR1.5: Ingest, validate, and abstract condition monitoring telemetry from Vehicle subsystems into a unified schema suitable for both real-time FDI and predictive maintenance model training.

assumptions: Sub-millisecond failover response time is a hard requirement derived from worksite safety SLAs (not explicitly stated in input but implied by 'two-tier architecture' mitigation).
Vehicle subsystems can host lightweight, stateless FDI agents that execute Safety Services policies without requiring bidirectional state synchronization.
Network latency between Vehicle and Safety Services is non-deterministic and cannot be relied upon for safety-critical failover decisions.
Predictive maintenance models are owned and trained by Safety Services; Vehicle provides only raw condition data feeds.
Teleoperation override authority is constrained by Safety Services veto on safety-critical actions; escalation paths are defined in Tasking interface contract.
Availability measurement (≥0.97) is aggregated across all redundancy, prevention, and recovery mechanisms and is observable via operational telemetry.
open_risks: Risk: Two-tier FDI architecture introduces state consistency challenges between local Vehicle agents and centralized Safety Services. Mitigation strategy needed for handling split-brain scenarios (e.g., Vehicle detects fault but Safety Services is unreachable).

Risk: Condition monitoring data schema evolution may require backward compatibility across Vehicle firmware versions and Safety Services model versions. Schema governance and versioning strategy must be defined.
Risk: Predictive maintenance model accuracy depends on sufficient historical condition data; cold-start problem may delay availability gains in early deployment phases.

Risk: Tasking interface escalation paths may introduce human-in-the-loop latency that conflicts with sub-millisecond failover SLA. Clarification needed on which failover decisions are automatic vs. escalated.
Risk: Local FDI agents on Vehicle may diverge from Safety Services policy if update mechanisms are not robust. Policy synchronization and rollback procedures must be defined.
evidence_needed: Latency analysis: Measure end-to-end failover response time with centralized Safety Services FDI vs. two-tier architecture. Confirm sub-millisecond SLA is achievable.
Network reliability data: Characterize Vehicle-to-Safety Services communication latency distribution, packet loss rates, and failure modes to justify two-tier partitioning.
Condition monitoring schema specification: Define unified telemetry schema for Vehicle subsystems (e.g., motor current, temperature, vibration) and map to predictive maintenance feature extraction.
Predictive maintenance model validation: Demonstrate model accuracy (e.g., false positive rate, lead time to failure prediction) on representative historical datasets.
Tasking interface contract: Document failover decision escalation matrix (automatic vs. escalated) and Safety Services veto authority constraints.
State consistency protocol: Define mechanism for Vehicle local FDI agents to synchronize with Safety Services global state (e.g., heartbeat, eventual consistency model).
Operational acceptance criteria: Confirm that ≥0.97 availability target is achievable with two-tier architecture and define measurement methodology (e.g., MTBF, MTTR, coverage metrics).
